event: Bronze Age settlements of the Umm an-Nar and Hafit cultures flourish, trading copper from the land the Sumerians called Magan.
Mesopotamian records identify Magan (likely Oman) as a key source of copper and diorite for the cities of Sumer and Akkad.
Iron Age communities develop the falaj irrigation systems that channel mountain water to oasis agriculture.
Oman comes under the influence of the Achaemenid Persian Empire, which establishes coastal settlements and control over trade.
Arab tribes from Yemen, notably the Azd, begin migrating into Oman, shaping its tribal and cultural identity.
The Sasanian Persian Empire reasserts control over coastal Oman, garrisoning the region and dominating Gulf trade.
Oman converts to Islam during the lifetime of the Prophet Muhammad after envoys reach the ruling Julanda brothers.
Omanis establish the first Ibadi Imamate, electing al-Julanda ibn Mas'ud as Imam and founding a distinctive religious-political tradition.
An Abbasid army crushes the Imamate at the Battle of Samad, briefly bringing Oman back under caliphal authority.
The Nabhani dynasty rises to power, ruling much of Oman as muluk (kings) alongside the persisting Ibadi imamate tradition.
Portuguese commander Afonso de Albuquerque captures Muscat and other coastal towns, beginning over a century of Portuguese occupation.
Nasir bin Murshid is elected Imam, founding the Yaruba dynasty that unites Oman and challenges Portuguese power.
Imam Sultan bin Saif expels the Portuguese from Muscat, ending European colonial control of the Omani coast.
Omani forces capture Mombasa and Fort Jesus, expanding Omani power along the East African coast and Zanzibar.
Ahmad bin Said is elected Imam, founding the Al Said dynasty that still rules Oman today.
Oman signs a treaty of friendship with Britain, the first of several agreements drawing it into the British sphere.
Said bin Sultan begins his long reign, building Oman into a major maritime and commercial empire spanning the Indian Ocean.
Said bin Sultan moves his capital to Zanzibar, making the East African island the center of his prosperous trading empire.
On Said bin Sultan's death the empire is divided, separating the Sultanate of Zanzibar from the Sultanate of Muscat and Oman.
Muscat and Oman becomes a British protectorate under a treaty granting Britain strong influence over its foreign affairs.
Interior tribes revolt and revive the Ibadi Imamate, opening a long rift between the coastal Sultanate and the inland Imamate.
The Treaty of Seeb grants the interior Imamate autonomy under the Sultan's nominal sovereignty, freezing the divide for decades.
Said bin Taimur becomes Sultan, beginning a reign marked by isolation, austerity, and resistance to modernization.
Discovery of oil prospects reignites conflict as the Sultan moves to assert control over the interior and its Imamate.
The Jebel Akhdar War erupts as the Sultan, backed by Britain, fights to suppress the rebellious interior Imamate.
British and Sultanate forces defeat the Imamate rebels on the Jebel Akhdar, ending the interior's autonomous rule.
Commercial quantities of oil are discovered, setting the stage for Oman's economic transformation.
The Dhofar Rebellion begins in the south, as leftist insurgents wage a guerrilla war against the Sultanate.
Oman begins exporting crude oil for the first time, inaugurating an era of petroleum-based revenue.
Qaboos bin Said overthrows his father in a palace coup and launches sweeping modernization, renaming the country the Sultanate of Oman.
Oman joins the United Nations and the Arab League, ending its long international isolation.
With Iranian and British support, Sultan Qaboos defeats the Dhofar Rebellion, ending the decade-long insurgency.
Oman becomes a founding member of the Gulf Cooperation Council, deepening regional economic and security ties.
Sultan Qaboos issues the Basic Law, Oman's first written constitution, codifying governance and succession.
Oman holds its first universal-suffrage elections for the consultative Majlis al-Shura.
Oman signs a free trade agreement with the United States, expanding its economic diversification efforts.
Arab Spring protests in Sohar and elsewhere prompt Sultan Qaboos to announce reforms, jobs, and expanded council powers.
Oman hosts secret talks that help broker the Iran nuclear deal, cementing its role as a regional mediator.
Sultan Qaboos dies after a 50-year reign and is succeeded by his cousin Haitham bin Tariq Al Said.
Sultan Haitham issues a new succession-defining Basic Law and names a crown prince for the first time in Oman's modern history.
